mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series 628-635CSi engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M30 engine typically costs £3,200–£6,500 supply only depending on the variant, with the 635CSi's M30B34 sitting at £4,000–£6,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M30 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M30 'big six' is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200,000+ miles is routine with proper maintenance. However, age-related issues are well-documented: head gasket failure (particularly on higher-mileage engines), oil leaks from the valve cover and oil pan, timing chain tensioner wear, and cooling system component failure. Regular maintenance — especially valve adjustments — is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the 628-635CSi?

acceptedAnswer.text: Head gasket failure (the most significant issue, especially on early 1977–80 cars with EGR-induced warping), oil leaks (valve cover and oil pan gaskets), timing chain and tensioner wear (rattle on cold start), cooling system failures (water pump and thermostat), and broken rocker arms or worn valve adjusters. See the Era Map above for variant-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an M30 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-maintained E24 6 Series (values £8,000–£35,000+ depending on variant and condition), a £3,500–£5,000 reconditioned engine is 15–60% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 628-635CSi engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code (M30B28, M30B30, M30B32, or M30B34) is stamped on the cylinder block. As a rule of thumb: 630CS (1976–1979) = M30B30; 628CSi (1979–1987) = M30B28; 633CSi (1978–1984) = M30B32; 635CSi (1978–1989) = M30B34. Check your VIN plate and vehicle documentation for confirmation.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M30 head gasket, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Head gasket failure is repairable without replacing the engine — if caught early. A head gasket replacement with head skimming costs £800–£1,800. Early (1977–80) M30s with EGR-induced warping may require an updated cylinder head. If the head is cracked or the engine has overheated severely, a full engine replacement (£3,200–£6,500) may be required.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the E24 M30 era (1976–1989). Different engine capacities command different pricing — see the Era Map above for variant-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an M30 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The M30 is relatively straightforward to work on, but classic car specialists are recommended. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: Cylinder Head Gasket Failure (M30, All Variants)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, white smoke from exhaust, oil in coolant or coolant in oil, misfiring, loss of power.
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,800 (head gasket replacement, head skimming if warped)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see signs of head gasket failure, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: Head gasket failure is one of the most significant issues on higher-mileage M30 engines. Early cars (1977–1980) are particularly vulnerable — a horrible EGR system created excessive heat and warped cylinder heads, leading to a class-action lawsuit and BMW replacing most heads with an updated design that did not warp. Failing head gaskets have become a known issue as the engine has aged, particularly around cylinder 6. If you notice coolant loss, overheating, or white smoke, have the head gasket tested immediately. Proactive replacement of the cooling system (water pump, thermostat, hoses) can help prevent overheating that causes head gasket failure.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover & Oil Pan Gaskets)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve cover or oil pan, low oil level warning.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 (valve cover gasket or oil pan gasket replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are among the most common issues with the M30, particularly from the valve cover gasket and oil pan. Over time, gaskets can wear out, leading to leaks that can create a mess and potentially affect engine performance. The M30 engine is very durable if well maintained. The problem is, most aren't. Regular inspections can catch these before they become major issues. If you notice oil spots under the car or a burning smell, have it inspected promptly.

problems.title: Timing Chain & Tensioner Wear (M30)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the front of the engine on cold start, metallic clattering, loss of power, potential engine failure if chain skips or snaps.
problems.replacementCost: £3,200–£5,500 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M30's timing chain is generally durable, but the chain will stretch with time, and the spring in the tensioner will get weak. After 250,000 km (approx 155,000 miles), the timing chain may require replacement. Symptoms of wear include metallic sounds on startup. If the chain skips a tooth, the engine can suffer from bent valves and very low compression. A cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes and maintaining correct oil pressure are the best prevention.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (Water Pump & Thermostat)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature fluctuations, sweet smell from engine bay, low coolant warning.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to head gasket damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss or overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: The water pump and thermostat will eventually fail — though less frequently than on newer models. The M30's cooling system is relatively simple but age takes its toll. If you notice coolant loss or temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system pressure-tested immediately. Proactive replacement of the water pump, thermostat and hoses at 100,000-mile intervals is recommended preventative maintenance.

problems.title: Broken Rocker Arms & Worn Valve Adjusters
problems.symptoms: Tapping or ticking noise from the top of the engine, loss of power, rough running, metallic clattering.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless failure leads to valve damage)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can lead to valve damage

problems.recommendation: The M30's valvetrain uses rocker arms and adjustable valve lash. Most everything in the head can cause problems: the occasional broken rocker (more likely if you run at high RPM a lot), worn adjusters. Failure to adjust valves and a loose cam oiler bar results in a worn camshaft. Regular valve adjustments (every 15,000–20,000 miles) are essential for the M30. If you hear tapping from the top of the engine, have the valve clearances checked and adjusted.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 6 Series 628–635CSi — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it before head warps or cracks; early M30s (1977–80) need updated heads
Catch it before chain snaps; proactive replacement at 150k+ miles
Straightforward replacement; catch it before overheating
Valve adjustment is routine; rocker replacement is manageable
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 628–635CSi? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with documented valve adjustments (every 15–20k miles)
* Cooling system in good order (recent water pump, thermostat, hoses)
* No signs of head gasket failure (no coolant loss, no overheating, no white smoke)
* No cold-start rattle (timing chain tensioner should be healthy)
* Early cars (1977–80): updated cylinder head fitted (EGR warping issue resolved)
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — the M30 is durable but unforgiving of neglect
* Signs of head gasket failure — expensive repair ahead
* Cold-start rattle present — timing chain work is imminent
* Any overheating history — potential head warping or gasket damage
* Excessive oil consumption or leaks — may indicate worn piston rings or valve seals

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 628–635CSi represents a golden era for BMW — the original 'shark nose' 6 Series, powered by the legendary M30 'big six'. This is one of the most durable engines BMW ever built, with many examples exceeding 200,000 miles. However, age takes its toll: head gasket failure, oil leaks, timing chain wear, and cooling system issues are the key watchpoints. Buy on service history, maintain on a regular schedule (especially valve adjustments), and this is one of the most rewarding classic BMWs you can own. The M30 engine is very durable if well maintained. The problem is, most aren't.

cta.label: → Compare 628–635CSi engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 628–635CSi (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle from the front of the engine (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Service History — Valve adjustments every 15–20k miles are essential for the M30
* Head Gasket — Any signs of coolant loss, overheating, or white smoke? (head gasket failure is common)
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve cover and oil pan
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates cooling system issues
* Engine Temperature — Any fluctuations? Water pump or thermostat may be failing
* Valve Train — Any tapping or ticking from the top of the engine?
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to fuel system or EGR issues on early cars)
